Site Updates Using Copilot

astro-development
Published on Jun 12, 2026 byLehman Î‡ 6 min read

Table of Contents

Show more
  • Introduction
  • The Prompt
  • Results
  • Discussion
  • Conclusions

Introduction

Iâ€™ve been using VS Code for awhile now, but I disabled the Github Copilot Chat extension, as I was finding the autocompletion feature to be a bit annoying.

I considered adding a couple more sections to the About page, but as the page was already a bit long I decided to modify the sections to expand and collapse, with collapse being the default for some (but not all) of the sections. That sounded like a good experiment for using Copilot Chatâ€¦

The Prompt

Confession: This was my second attempt with Copilot. The first time around, I forgot to add the tailwind styling stipulation. Copilot used a vanilla-flavored details tag for the sections, which worked, but had no styling added. I requested that addition, and Copilot added the tailwind CSS stuff. Overall though, it felt klunky, so I backed out all of the changes, and started a new Copilot chat with the prompt as shown above.

Lesson Learned: Think about what you really want and make the prompt as detailed as required.

Results

This time around Copilot created a Details component and used that in the About page. Clever. But it didnâ€™t correctly detect the light and dark mode available for the site. As a result, text was not readable in dark mode. I had Copilot fix that successfully. But wait - the link text was still washed out. So one more iteration was required, and Copilot figured out how to make the link text visible.

The complete conversation is shown below. (Click on â€ś128 collapsed linesâ€ť to view the complete file.)

Lesson Learned: Test out the new feature, including all of the user interface options.

Discussion

I was impressed with how Copilot was able to walk through the Astro code hierarchy to find and adjust the appropriate CSS settings. An experienced Astro developer (that is not me, BTW) would have probably figured that out pretty quickly. Creating a Details component was a very good Astro-centric way of handling a basic HTML tag like details (and frankly, an element with which I was unfamiliar).

Conclusions

I have disabled Copilotâ€™s autocompletion feature in VS Code, but I expect Iâ€™ll fire up Copilot Chat the next time I want to add something new to the site.
